docs.title: Home / Overview
docs.text: Welcome. This site is where I keep my working AI engineering playbook: what I build, what fails, and what actually works in production.

docs.title: What This Site Is
docs.text: A practical knowledge base for designing, building, and improving AI systems. It is intentionally implementation-first.

docs.text: Build small, measure early, keep systems observable, and prefer repeatable patterns over one-off cleverness.

docs.title: What is AI Engineering (Practical View)
docs.text: AI engineering is product engineering with probabilistic components. The goal is not just "make the model answer" but "make the system reliable, useful, and maintainable."

docs.title: LLM Basics (Only What Matters in Practice)
docs.text:
• LLMs are pattern predictors, not truth engines
  • Prompt quality directly affects output quality
  • Retrieval quality often matters more than prompt cleverness
  • Guardrails and validation are part of the product, not optional extras

docs.title: Tokens, Context, and Limitations
docs.text:
• Tokens are cost, latency, and capacity units
  • Context windows are limited, so selection is everything
  • Bigger prompts can reduce clarity when irrelevant content is included

docs.title: Embeddings Explained Simply
docs.text: Embeddings convert text into vectors so we can search by meaning instead of exact wording.

docs.title: Retrieval vs Fine-Tuning vs Prompting
docs.text:
• Prompting: first step for behavior shaping
  • Retrieval (RAG): best for fresh, private, or large knowledge
  • Fine-tuning: useful when style/behavior consistency is required at scale

docs.title: When NOT to Use AI
docs.text: Skip AI when deterministic rules solve the task better, correctness must be exact every time, or operational risk outweighs value.

docs.title: Architecture Diagrams
docs.text: High-level system maps and flow views live here.

docs.title: System View
docs.text:
flowchart TD
  U[User] --> API[Flask API]
  API --> ORCH[Orchestration Layer]
  ORCH --> RET[Retrieval Layer]
  ORCH --> LLM[Model Provider]
  RET --> KB[Knowledge Base]
  ORCH --> OBS[Logs and Evaluation]

docs.title: AI Application Engineering Development Plan (6-12 Months)
Objective
docs.text:
To formalize and advance my skills in AI-driven application engineering, with a focus on building secure, scalable, and production-ready internal systems using Python, Flask, Docker, and locally hosted AI models.

This direction aligns with my current work developing internal tools and positions me to deliver higher-value, future-ready solutions for the business.

docs.title: 1. Retrieval-Augmented AI Systems (RAG)
docs.text:
Goal: Improve the accuracy, reliability, and usefulness of AI-powered internal tools.

Key skills:

  • Document preprocessing (chunking, metadata strategies)
  • Embeddings and semantic search
  • Hybrid retrieval and reranking
  • Source attribution and citation
  • Retrieval quality evaluation

Outcome: Development of robust internal knowledge systems that provide accurate, traceable, and context-aware responses.

docs.title: 2. Local AI Model Deployment
docs.text:
Goal: Build secure, in-house AI capabilities independent of external services.

Key skills:

  • Running and managing local LLMs (for example, Ollama or similar)
  • Model selection and performance trade-offs
  • Embedding vs generation models
  • API-based model serving
  • Resource optimization (CPU/GPU, memory, latency)

Outcome: Deployment of private, cost-controlled AI systems suitable for sensitive or internal data use cases.

docs.title: 3. AI System Evaluation and Reliability
docs.text:
Goal: Ensure AI systems are measurable, consistent, and production-ready.

Key skills:

  • Creating evaluation datasets
  • Measuring answer accuracy and relevance
  • Detecting hallucinations and failure cases
  • Regression testing across model and prompt changes
  • Logging and observability

Outcome: AI applications that are testable, reliable, and maintainable over time.

docs.title: 4. Tool-Integrated AI Workflows
docs.text:
Goal: Extend AI systems beyond Q&A into actionable workflows.

Key skills:

  • Designing safe tool interfaces (APIs, scripts, internal services)
  • Structured outputs and schema design
  • Workflow orchestration (lightweight agents where appropriate)
  • Understanding emerging standards (for example, MCP)

Outcome: AI-enabled tools that can interact with internal systems and automate tasks, not just generate responses.

docs.title: Phase 1 (0-3 months)
docs.text:
• Enhance existing RAG system
  • Improve retrieval quality and structure
  • Add citation and evaluation capabilities
  • Begin local model experimentation and benchmarking

docs.title: Phase 2 (3-6 months)
docs.text:
• Deploy a stable local model service
  • Introduce an evaluation framework for AI outputs
  • Optimize performance and reliability of existing tools

docs.title: Phase 3 (6-12 months)
docs.text:
• Integrate tool-based workflows into AI systems
  • Expand use cases (automation, reporting, internal tooling)
  • Refine architecture for scalability and maintainability

docs.title: Business Value
docs.text:
• Enables secure, in-house AI solutions (reduced reliance on external APIs)
  • Improves internal knowledge access and productivity
  • Supports development of custom AI-powered tools tailored to business needs
  • Builds capability in a rapidly growing and strategically important area
  • Ensures solutions are maintainable, measurable, and production-ready

docs.title: Training and Learning Approach
docs.text:
• Targeted online courses in AI engineering (RAG systems, applied LLMs)
  • Targeted online courses in backend and API development (advanced Python)
  • Hands-on implementation through existing and new internal projects
  • Selective study of emerging standards and tools (for example, MCP, local model frameworks)

docs.title: Evidence from Current Projects
docs.text:
The development plan is already being applied through active internal project work:

  • MVP Internal RAG: containerized local-model RAG assistant using Flask/FastAPI, Ollama, and Qdrant with citation-backed responses.
  • docx2html Pipeline: production-focused document conversion and content assembly workflow with validation and optional AI-assisted enrichment.

These projects provide practical delivery context for the focus areas above and create a measurable base for the next 6-12 months of capability growth.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This plan builds on my existing strengths in Python-based application development and aligns my growth with modern AI engineering practices. It focuses on practical, high-impact skills that support both current responsibilities and future business needs.

docs.title: Embeddings
docs.text: Vector representations used to compare meaning between text segments.

docs.title: Tokens
docs.text: Units of text processed and generated by language models.

docs.title: Context Window
docs.text: Maximum input span a model can consider in one request.

docs.title: Hallucination
docs.text: A plausible-sounding output that is unsupported by evidence or incorrect.

docs.title: RAG Mistakes
docs.text: I underestimated how often retrieval quality, not model quality, was the root issue.

docs.title: Prompting Misconceptions
docs.text: Long prompts did not consistently improve quality. Clear constraints and format expectations did.

docs.title: Overengineering Traps
docs.text: Adding orchestration complexity too early slowed feedback loops and hid product priorities.

docs.title: What I'd Do Differently Now
docs.text: Start lean, instrument from day one, and only add complexity after repeated evidence.

docs.title: Things I Want to Explore
docs.text:
• Stronger automated evaluation loops
  • Better retrieval diagnostics and tuning
  • More robust multi-tool orchestration

docs.title: Ideas Not Yet Built
docs.text:
• Knowledge quality scoring service
  • Prompt/version experimentation dashboard
  • Agentic documentation operations assistant

docs.title: Skills to Develop Next
docs.text:
• Evaluation design discipline
  • Advanced retrieval methods
  • Production observability for AI systems

docs.title: Python & Flask Setup
docs.text: Python powers core logic. Flask keeps service APIs lightweight and easy to iterate.

docs.title: Docker Setup
docs.text: Use containers for repeatable local environments and deployment parity.

docs.title: Bash Automation
docs.text: Automate ingestion, testing, and benchmark routines to reduce manual drift.

docs.title: AI Libraries (What I Actually Use)
docs.text: Document concrete libraries and versions that are active in current projects.

docs.title: Dev Workflow
docs.text: Prototype fast, instrument early, test with real workloads, and refine based on observed failures.

docs.title: Experiments & Findings
docs.text: This is my lab notebook for controlled tests and practical conclusions.

docs.title: Experiment Pages
docs.text:
• RAG vs No RAG
  • Model Comparison
  • Chunk Size Testing

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Find chunking settings that improve retrieval quality.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text: Evaluate multiple chunk size/overlap strategies against a fixed query set.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: Medium, structure-aware chunks performed best on this corpus.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: Chunking should match document structure and query behavior, then be revalidated over time.

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Compare models against quality, latency, and operational cost targets.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text: Run the same benchmark prompts and constraints across selected models.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: No single model wins every category; trade-offs are workload-specific.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: Choose models by task profile and constraints, not benchmark headlines.

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Measure the quality impact of retrieval augmentation.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text: Same model and prompt set; compare direct generation versus retrieval-augmented generation.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: RAG improved factual grounding on domain-specific questions.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: Use RAG when answer quality depends on external or private knowledge.

docs.title: Why Local Models Matter
docs.text: Local models give tighter data control, predictable availability, and flexibility for internal workflows.

docs.title: Trade-offs vs API Models
docs.text:
• Pros: privacy, control, offline capability, potentially lower recurring cost
  • Cons: hardware constraints, model variance, deployment/ops overhead

docs.title: Setup
docs.text: Document install steps, model pulls, and runtime config so the environment is repeatable.

docs.title: Model Comparisons
docs.text: Compare models using the same prompt sets and constraints to avoid biased conclusions.

docs.title: Performance Notes
docs.text: Record context length, quantization, and hardware with each test so results are interpretable.

docs.title: CPU vs GPU Observations
docs.text: CPU is simpler to start with. GPU is the practical path for lower latency and higher throughput.

docs.title: Memory Usage
docs.text: Track RAM/VRAM usage by model size and quantization, especially under realistic context lengths.

docs.title: Latency Benchmarks
docs.text: Measure time-to-first-token and tokens-per-second for end-to-end comparisons.

docs.title: Patterns & Recipes
docs.text: This section is my reusable toolkit for AI implementation.

docs.title: Pattern Library
docs.text:
• RAG Pipeline
  • Flask AI App Structure
  • Prompt Templates
  • Tool Calling Patterns

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: AI app code becomes brittle when routing, prompting, model calls, and data access are mixed.

docs.title: Pattern
docs.text: Separate concerns: routes, orchestration services, model adapters, retrieval/data layer, and evaluation helpers.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: /chat route calls an orchestrator, which gets context from retrieval and selects a model adapter.

docs.title: When to Use / Avoid
docs.text: Use for production paths. Avoid excessive structure in quick throwaway prototypes.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Free-form prompts drift and produce inconsistent outputs.

docs.title: Pattern
docs.text: Use parameterized templates with explicit sections: role, task, constraints, expected format, and examples.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: A grounded QA template requiring source-backed answers and an uncertainty note.

docs.title: When to Use / Avoid
docs.text: Use for repeatable workflows. Avoid over-constraining pure ideation tasks.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Need reliable, grounded answers from a knowledge base.

docs.title: Pattern
docs.text: Ingest -> chunk -> embed -> retrieve -> rerank -> generate -> evaluate.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: Use hybrid retrieval and enforce a response format that cites retrieved evidence.

docs.title: When to Use / Avoid
docs.text: Use for knowledge-heavy assistants. Avoid when deterministic lookup logic is enough.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Tool orchestration fails when schemas are loose and validation is weak.

docs.title: Pattern
docs.text: Define strict tool schemas, validate arguments, add retries for transient failures, and provide fallbacks.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: Planner selects tool -> validator checks args -> executor runs tool -> summarizer composes final response.

docs.title: When to Use / Avoid
docs.text: Use in multi-step assistants. Avoid when a direct model response is simpler and sufficient.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: Project pages capture what I built, why I built it, and what I would change next.

docs.title: Current Project Pages
docs.text:
• MVP Internal RAG
  • docx2html Pipeline
  • Internal RAG System

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The current docx2html project is a Flask-based documentation production pipeline that combines DOCX conversion with a webcontent workflow. It produces normalized markdown, per-tutorial master files, and packaging outputs for publishing.

docs.title: Problem It Solves
docs.text:
• Source material is spread across tutorial web pages, ZIP assets, JSON payloads, and Word templates.
  • Manual content assembly creates drift and inconsistent output structure.
  • Publishing requires repeatable outputs with traceable intermediate artifacts.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
• Flask UI plus job-driven API endpoints for long-running pipeline actions
  • Core stages:
  • get_tutorial_assets.py: discover tutorials, download ZIPs, extract JSON and Word assets
  • extract_k2container.py: extract tutorial body content into webcontent/extracted_html
  • Conversion: docx -> html -> md plus extracted HTML/TXT to markdown
  • build_master_docs.py: build webcontent/Master/*-master.md from web markdown + docx markdown + JSON
  • Combine Masters: generate webcontent/Master/all-masters.md
  • Optional AI generation step creates webcontent/ai_content/*-content.txt (meta description + intro text)
  • Docker runtime on port 8006 with mounted input, output, and webcontent volumes

docs.title: Key Decisions
docs.text:
• Keep deterministic conversion as the default path and use AI as an optional constrained pass.
  • Preserve intermediate files (master_inputs, extracted assets, reports) for auditability and debugging.
  • Add settings-based URL override mode to run targeted subsets instead of full tutorial batches.
  • Separate cleanup and packaging into explicit operations to keep runs reproducible.

docs.title: Challenges
docs.text:
• Maintaining structure fidelity across DOCX, scraped web content, and JSON data.
  • Handling inconsistent source formats and noisy embedded data (for example base64 image payloads).
  • Balancing throughput with validation quality for batch documentation workflows.

docs.title: What I'd Improve
docs.text:
• Add stronger regression baselines for markdown structure and front matter consistency.
  • Introduce per-template conversion heuristics for known edge cases.
  • Add summary dashboards for job outcomes, warnings, and recurring failure categories.

docs.title: Notable Implementation Details
docs.text:
• Supports both full pipeline mode and direct folder conversion (Docx tab).
  • Generates validation reports in output/reports for conversion quality checks.
  • Adds front matter fields (title, description, canonical_url, last_reviewed) during master build.
  • Includes cleanup for generated artifacts while preserving source code and settings.
  • Supports downloadable outputs for markdown, master files, and AI content bundles.

docs.title: Optional OpenAI Summary Flow
docs.text:
When the user enables AI generation in Settings, the pipeline can call the OpenAI API to generate a concise summary package from markdown that has already been produced by earlier steps.

  • Input: previously generated master markdown files in webcontent/Master/*-master.md
  • Trigger: user turns on AI generation and runs the AI Generate action
  • API configuration: OPENAI_API_KEY plus selected model (default configured as gpt-4.1-mini)
  • Behavior: each master markdown file is processed individually and prompts request short summary-oriented content (for example, meta description and intro text)
  • Output: generated summary content is written to webcontent/ai_content/*-content.txt

This keeps the core conversion deterministic while allowing an opt-in, API-based summarization pass for publishing-ready metadata and concise introductions.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: A private assistant for internal docs and operational knowledge.

docs.title: Problem It Solves
docs.text: It reduces time spent searching fragmented documentation and improves response consistency.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: Ingestion -> chunking/embedding -> retrieval/reranking -> generation with source-aware output.

docs.title: Key Decisions
docs.text:
• Hybrid retrieval over vector-only
  • Structured prompts with grounding constraints
  • Retrieval and answer telemetry for diagnosis

docs.title: Challenges
docs.text:
• Recall vs precision tuning
  • Stale content handling
  • Source attribution reliability

docs.title: What I'd Improve
docs.text:
• Automated eval gates in CI
  • Better chunking heuristics by document type
  • More visible retrieval diagnostics

docs.title: Screenshots / Flow
docs.text: Add current UI and pipeline screenshots as the system evolves.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: MVP Internal RAG is a containerized internal AI support assistant built to run fully in-house. It provides grounded, citation-backed answers from internal documentation using a local model stack.

docs.title: Problem It Solves
docs.text:
• Internal support knowledge was spread across markdown, text, and PDF sources.
  • Answers needed to be traceable to source documents, not just generated from model memory.
  • The solution needed to run without relying on external AI APIs.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
• Service stack: Flask/FastAPI app + Ollama + Qdrant via Docker Compose
  • Ingestion flow: load docs -> chunk text -> embed chunks -> upsert into Qdrant
  • Query flow: embed question -> retrieve candidates -> rerank -> generate grounded answer
  • UI/API supports retrieval controls such as top_k, strictness, score thresholds, and answer style

docs.title: Key Decisions
docs.text:
• Use RAG over fine-tuning for maintainability and faster iteration.
  • Keep models local (dolphin3 for generation, nomic-embed-text for embeddings).
  • Enforce source-aware responses with explicit citation formatting.
  • Add strict mode and minimum semantic threshold to reduce weak-context answers.

docs.title: Challenges
docs.text:
• Balancing recall and precision for documentation-heavy queries.
  • Keeping chunk size and overlap effective across different document formats.
  • Preventing plausible but weakly supported responses in edge cases.

docs.title: What I'd Improve
docs.text:
• Add automated regression tests for retrieval and answer quality.
  • Expand observability on retrieval misses and reranking behavior.
  • Introduce per-document chunking profiles for better retrieval consistency.

docs.title: Notable Implementation Details
docs.text:
• Endpoints include /ingest, /chat, /ask, /find, and /health.
  • Supports page-aware citations for PDF-derived chunks.
  • Uses deterministic point IDs for stable Qdrant upserts.
  • Containerized deployment supports local GPU acceleration where available.

docs.title: What is RAG (Real-World Explanation)
docs.text: RAG means retrieving relevant knowledge first, then generating an answer grounded in that evidence.

docs.title: When RAG Works Well / Fails
docs.text: RAG works well when knowledge is large, private, or frequently updated. It fails when chunking is poor, retrieval is weak, or source quality is inconsistent.

docs.title: My RAG Architecture (Diagram Optional)
docs.text:
flowchart LR
  A[User Query] --> B[Query Processing]
  B --> C[Retriever]
  C --> D[Top-K Context]
  D --> E[LLM Generation]
  E --> F[Answer with Sources]

Use the pages below for implementation detail.

docs.title: Chunking Strategies
docs.text:
• Fixed-size chunks with overlap
  • Structure-aware chunks (headings/sections)
  • Hybrid chunking for mixed technical content

docs.title: Trade-offs (Size vs Context)
docs.text:
• Smaller chunks improve precision
  • Larger chunks improve continuity
  • Overlap helps recall but increases storage and noise

docs.title: What Worked for Me
docs.text: Heading-aware chunking with moderate overlap has been the best default. I tune from retrieval misses, not guesswork.

docs.title: How I Test RAG Systems
docs.text:
• Build a representative query set
  • Score retrieval relevance before answer quality
  • Check groundedness against retrieved evidence
  • Track failure categories over time

docs.title: Common Failure Cases
docs.text:
• Confident but unsupported answers
  • Missing key chunk in top-k retrieval
  • Wrong source selected due to lexical noise

docs.title: Metrics That Matter
docs.text:
• Top-k relevance quality
  • Grounded answer rate
  • Citation usefulness
  • Latency and cost per request

docs.title: Vector Search Basics
docs.text: Vector retrieval finds semantically related text even when the query words do not exactly match source wording.

docs.title: Hybrid Search
docs.text: Hybrid retrieval combines vector similarity with keyword search for stronger recall and precision.

docs.title: Reranking
docs.text: Reranking improves final context quality by promoting the most relevant chunks before generation.
